1.exposition: The Bible's final movement opens not with an ending but with a beginning. John sees a new heaven and a new earth, the old order gone and the sea with it; then the holy city, new Jerusalem, descending like a bride adorned for her husband. A great voice announces the covenant's consummation — the dwelling of God is with man — and with his own hand God wipes away every tear: no more death, mourning, crying, or pain. From the throne comes the great word, Behold, I am making all things new, sealed by the one who is Alpha and Omega with the free gift of the water of life. The overcomer inherits all things and is called God's son (v.7); only the unrepentant, headed by the cowardly, are shut out (v.8). This is Scripture's answer to Genesis 3: not a world discarded but a world healed, every wound of the fall reversed.
1.original_language: The "new" of v.1 is kainos — new in kind and quality, not merely neos, new in time: the same creation made gloriously other, as a body is raised. The hinge word of the chapter is skēnē (v.3), the tabernacle or dwelling-tent — God's presence pitched among his people. The same root gave John his earlier line that the Word eskēnōsen, "tabernacled," among us (John 1:14); here that dwelling becomes permanent. And gegonan, "It is done", answers the tetelestai of the cross (John 19:30) — what was finished there is now fully done.
1.historical_context: Every line here gathers up the prophets. The new heaven and new earth fulfill Isaiah 65:17 and Isaiah 66:22; the wiped-away tears quote Isaiah 25:8; the covenant formula, "they shall be his people," runs from Leviticus 26:11–12 through Ezekiel 37:27 and Jeremiah 31:33. In the ancient Near Eastern imagination the sea was the realm of chaos and the monster's home (Revelation 13:1); its absence signals chaos undone. The free water of life echoes the great open-handed invitation of Isaiah 55:1.
1.christ: The consummation is the fruit of the cross. The "It is done" from the throne is the public unfolding of Christ's "It is finished" (John 19:30); the same title Alpha and Omega spoken here of God is claimed by the risen Christ in 22:13, so that Father and Son together are the source and goal of all things. The bride descending (v.2) is the Lamb's bride announced at the marriage supper (19:7) — the community bought with his blood. And the living water freely given (v.6) is the very gift Christ pressed on the woman at the well (John 4:14).

verses.note: "I saw a new heaven and a new earth" — and the word is kainos, new in quality. grammatical-historical Barnes and the latin-fathers Victorinus take the first heaven and earth to pass not by annihilation but by renovation, the present world purged by fire and changed for the better (2 Peter 3:13); reformed Gill calls it a change of quality, not of substance. The sea — in Scripture the emblem of restless chaos and the turbulent nations — is "no more," its unrest forever stilled (Isaiah 65:17).

Then the vision every exile longs for: the holy city, new Jerusalem, coming down out of heaven from God, "prepared as a bride adorned for her husband." grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note that she descends — she is God's gift, not humanity's achievement (Galatians 4:26; Hebrews 11:10) — and that the bride is the deliberate counter-image to the harlot Babylon: two cities, two women, the one arising from the beast, the other coming down from God.

The loud voice announces the goal of the whole story: "Behold, the dwelling of God is with man" — skēnē, the tabernacle at last pitched forever among his people. puritan Matthew Henry hears the ancient covenant fully accomplished: he will be their God and they his people (Leviticus 26:11–12; Ezekiel 37:27; Jeremiah 31:33). Everything from Eden onward — every tabernacle and temple — was reaching toward this one sentence.

And then the tenderest line in the book: "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes" (Isaiah 25:8). grammatical-historical Barnes marks that it is God's own hand that dries them. Death, mourning, crying, and pain — the fourfold weight of the curse — are gone, "for the former things have passed away": the precise, point-by-point reversal of the sorrows loosed in Genesis 3.

For the first time since the throne-room opened, the One seated there speaks directly: "Behold, I am making all things new" (2 Corinthians 5:17; Isaiah 43:19). grammatical-historical Robertson notes the present tense — a renewing already under way, not merely postponed. "Write, for these words are trustworthy and true": the promise is underwritten by God's own veracity, so that the weary may lean their whole weight on it.

"It is done. I am the Alpha and the Omega" (1:8). The single word gegonan echoes the seventh bowl's cry (16:17) and, behind it, the cross. reformed Gill dwells on the promise that follows: to the thirsty God gives "of the fountain of the water of life freely" (Isaiah 55:1; John 4:14) — the consummation is not earned but given.

"The one who conquers will inherit these things" — the promise that crowned each of the seven letters now opens onto the whole new creation. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the royal adoption formula once spoken to David's heir, "I will be his God and he shall be my son" (2 Samuel 7:14), here made the birthright of every overcomer (Romans 8:17).

The bright promise casts a shadow: the cowardly, the faithless, and the defiled have their part in the lake of fire, the second death (20:14). grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note that "the cowardly" head the list — those whose fear of man outweighed their faith. The catalogue is the deliberate dark foil to the overcomer of v.7: two destinies, and no third.

witnesses.point: The first heaven and earth pass not by annihilation but by renovation — purged and changed for the better, made the fit home of the redeemed.
A change of quality, not of substance; and the sea, symbol of the restless and turbulent, is no more.
The world passes "by the conflagration of the supernal fire," the earth changed for the better, not destroyed.
"He will be their God, and they shall be his people" — the covenant fully accomplished, and the fourfold curse of the fall undone.

external.name: Craig R. Koester
external.point: Reads the wiping of every tear as the vision's pastoral heart — a promise addressed to real grief, assuring those who suffer now that God himself will one day end their sorrow.

1.application: Notice where the whole story lands: not in our flight up to God but in God's descent down to us. The last word spoken over every grave, every hospital room, every buried hope, is a hand — God's own — reaching to wipe the face clean.

9.exposition: One of the seven angels who had poured out the bowls — the very kind who once showed John the harlot — now says, "Come, I will show you the bride, the wife of the Lamb", and carries him to a high mountain to see the holy city descending, radiant with the glory of God. What follows is an architect's tour: a great wall with twelve gates named for the twelve tribes and twelve foundations named for the twelve apostles; the city measured with a golden reed and found to be a perfect cube of immense dimension; walls of jasper, streets and city of transparent gold, foundations ablaze with twelve jewels, and gates each cut from a single pearl. The details are not a jeweler's inventory but a theology in stone: the bride is a city, the city is a people, and its cubic shape — the shape of the Holy of Holies — declares that the whole of it is the dwelling-place of God.
9.original_language: The angel offers to show the nymphē, the bride, and shows a polis, a city — the two images fused, for the people and their dwelling are one thing. The decisive term is the measure of v.16: the city's length, breadth, and height are isa, equal — a cube. No dwelling in Scripture was a perfect cube but one: the inner sanctuary of Solomon's temple, twenty cubits each way (1 Kings 6:20). The wall's 144 cubits (v.17) is twelve squared, the number of the covenant people; even the arithmetic preaches the totality of the redeemed.
9.historical_context: The whole vision is woven from Ezekiel and Isaiah. The Spirit-transport to a "great, high mountain" (v.10) reproduces Ezekiel 40:2, and the measuring angel with his reed comes straight from Ezekiel's temple-tour (Ezekiel 40:3; Zechariah 2:1). The twelve gates inscribed with the tribes fulfill Ezekiel 48:31–34; the jewelled foundations recall the twelve stones of the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17–20), on which the tribes were carried into God's presence. Ezekiel's great vision (Ezekiel 40–48) ended with a temple-city named "The LORD Is There"; John's ends with a city that needs no temple at all.
9.christ: The city is not merely God's but "the wife of the Lamb" — defined by her bridegroom, purchased at his cost. The twelve foundations bearing the apostles' names (v.14) are the church built on apostolic witness with Christ himself the cornerstone (Ephesians 2:20) — the very church he promised to build (Matthew 16:18). And the cube of v.16 is the cross's harvest: when the veil before the Holy of Holies was torn in two (Matthew 27:51), the way into God's presence was opened; here that presence has swallowed the whole city, so that all who are washed in the Lamb's blood live where once only the high priest could go.

verses.note: "Come, I will show you the bride, the wife of the Lamb." grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson catch the pointed symmetry: one of the seven bowl-angels who had shown John the great harlot (17:1) now shows him the bride. puritan Matthew Henry notes that city and bride are one figure (19:7) — the redeemed people of God in their final union with Christ.

Carried "in the Spirit to a great, high mountain", John is shown the holy city descending from God. grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson note the vantage is Ezekiel's exactly (Ezekiel 40:2): from the mountain the whole city can be taken in, and its descent marks it — once more — as heaven's gift, not earth's building.

The city has "the glory of God, its radiance like a most rare jewel, like a jasper, clear as crystal." eastern Cyril reads the light as the divine presence itself shining through her, the Shekinah made her lamp (v.23). Her brilliance is borrowed, not her own — God's glory transfiguring the bride into a flawless jewel.

A great, high wall with twelve gates, twelve angels at the gates, and the names of the twelve tribes inscribed. grammatical-historical Barnes notes the wall speaks security and the gates full access, while the tribal names bind the city to the covenant people of old (Ezekiel 48:31) — the redeemed of Israel written into the very entrances.

Three gates face each way — east, north, south, and west. reformed Gill hears the openness: the city is approachable from every quarter, and the redeemed will come "from east and west" to sit down in the kingdom (Matthew 8:11; Luke 13:29). No one saved is shut out by distance or direction.

Twelve foundations bear "the twelve names of the twelve apostles of the Lamb." grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the church is founded on the apostles (Ephesians 2:20); set with the tribal gates of v.12, the two together join the covenant's two epochs — Israel and the church — into one indivisible structure.

The angel has "a golden reed to measure the city", its gates and its wall. grammatical-historical Robertson recalls the measuring prophets before him (Ezekiel 40:3; Zechariah 2:1): to measure is to lay claim and to display perfection — nothing here is haphazard, nothing wanting.

The measure yields the vision's architectural climax: the city "lies foursquare," its length, breadth, and height equal — a perfect cube of twelve thousand stadia. reformed Gill sees the figure of complete and stable perfection, and behind it the one cube of Scripture, the Holy of Holies (1 Kings 6:20). The whole city is the sanctuary; all of it is the place of God's presence.

The wall measures 144 cubits, "by human measurement, which is also an angel's." grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the number is twelve squared — the tribes and apostles multiplied, the full tally of God's people (the 144,000 of 7:4) written even into the thickness of the wall.

"The wall was built of jasper, while the city was pure gold, clear as glass." reformed Gill dwells on the paradox — gold so refined it is transparent, a purity beyond anything earth mines. The jasper wall carries the city's own glory (v.11); the whole is beauty without flaw or shadow.

The foundations are "adorned with every kind of jewel" — jasper, sapphire, agate, emerald. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott hear the echo of the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17–20), where the tribes were borne in precious stones over the priest's heart; now the apostolic foundations blaze with the same manifold glory.

The list completes the twelve: onyx, carnelian, chrysolite, beryl, topaz, chrysoprase, jacinth, amethyst. grammatical-historical Barnes notes that many of the twelve answer to the stones of Israel's high priest (Exodus 28:17); the full spectrum of color, gathered into the foundations, is the manifold beauty of the one people of God.

The twelve gates are twelve pearls, each gate a single pearl, and the street is pure transparent gold. grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the pearl, the costliest of gems (Matthew 13:46): every entrance to the city is worth a kingdom, and the very ground the redeemed walk is gold as clear as light.

witnesses.point: The same bowl-angel who displayed the harlot now displays the bride — the deliberate contrast that crowns the book.
The city is figurative — the glorified church; its cubic form is the emblem of a perfection equal and stable in every dimension.
The church is founded on the apostles' doctrine, and its twelve gates stand open enough for all who have right to enter, from every nation.
The Spirit-transport to the mountain and the measuring reed reproduce Ezekiel's temple vision; the city is measured and found perfect.

external.name: Richard Bauckham
external.point: Argues that the New Jerusalem is at once a place and a people — bride and city fused — and that its enormous cube-shape identifies the whole city as the Holy of Holies, so that the presence of God, once confined to one sacred room, now fills the entire redeemed creation.

9.application: The measurements can feel cold until you see what they mean: there is room. Twelve gates on every side, foundations for all the tribes and all the apostles, a city vast beyond reckoning — this is the architecture of a welcome that leaves no one standing outside for lack of space.

22.pericope_label: The Glory of the New Jerusalem
22.exposition: The tour ends in three great absences, and each absence is really an arrival. John sees no temple — "for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb." There is no sun and no moon, for the glory of God is the city's light and the Lamb its lamp. There is no night, so the gates are never shut. Into that unfailing light the nations walk and the kings bring their glory, carrying the honor of the nations in through the open gates. And the chapter closes on its one condition: nothing unclean will ever enter, but only those written in the Lamb's book of life. No mediating temple, because God is immediately present; no created light, because uncreated glory shines; no night and no shut gate, because there is nothing left to fear.
22.original_language: John sees no naos (v.22) — no inner sanctuary — because God and the Lamb are the sanctuary. The Lamb is the city's lychnos (v.23), its "lamp": God's glory is the light, and the Lamb the lamp that bears it — the same word used of the golden lampstands among which Christ first walked (1:12). The gates "will never be shut" (v.25): in the ancient world a city's gates closed at dusk against danger, but where there is no night and no enemy, the bolt is never drawn.
22.historical_context: These verses are saturated with Isaiah 60, Jerusalem's great oracle of glory. The light that outshines sun and moon is Isaiah 60:19–20; the nations walking by that light, Isaiah 60:3; the kings bringing tribute, Isaiah 60:5; the gates that never close, Isaiah 60:11. The absence of a temple reverses the whole trajectory of the Old Testament — tabernacle, Solomon's temple, Ezekiel's visionary temple (Ezekiel 40–48) — because the reality every temple pointed to has finally come. The exclusion of the unclean echoes Isaiah 52:1 and Zechariah 14:21.
22.christ: Here the Lamb stands in the very place of God. "Its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb" (v.22); "its lamp is the Lamb" — the crucified one is the light of the whole city, fulfilling "I am the light of the world" (John 8:12) at cosmic scale. The kings who once brought their glory to Babylon (18:9) now bring it to the Lamb, before whom every knee bows (Philippians 2:10). And the register of citizens is "the Lamb's book of life": to belong to the city is simply to belong to him.

verses.note: "I saw no temple in the city, for its temple is the Lord God the Almighty and the Lamb." reformed Gill calls God and the Lamb the substance and antitype of which every temple was only the shadow; puritan Matthew Henry marks the meaning — the mediated presence of the old order gives way to God immediately present, so that no sacred building is needed, for now all is holy.

"The city has no need of sun or moon," for God's glory lights it and "its lamp is the Lamb." reformed Gill names the light the Shekinah, the divine presence itself (Isaiah 60:19–20); the created luminaries are outshone and made needless, and the redeemed walk forever in the light of God and of Christ (John 8:12).

"By its light will the nations walk, and the kings of the earth will bring their glory into it." eastern Cyril sees all earthly glory laid at God's feet in the holy city (Isaiah 60:3). These are the redeemed kings, the mirror-opposite of the kings who once trafficked with Babylon — homage now fills the streets where commerce once did.

"Its gates will never be shut by day — and there will be no night there." grammatical-historical Barnes notes that earthly cities bar their gates at nightfall against danger; this city has neither night nor enemy (Isaiah 60:11; 22:5), and so its gates stand open in unending, perfect welcome.

"They will bring into it the glory and the honor of the nations." grammatical-historical Barnes reads it as the best of every people and age gathered up and consecrated to God (Isaiah 60:5) — whatever is truly noble in human civilization finds its home and its purpose at last within the city of God.

The one wall that remains is moral: "nothing unclean will ever enter it," only "those who are written in the Lamb's book of life." grammatical-historical Barnes and Ellicott note the contrast with Babylon, full of abomination (17:4–5): the holy city is wholly clean (Isaiah 52:1), and its single gate of entry is grace — a name written in the Lamb's book (20:15).

witnesses.point: There is no temple, for God is immediately present; and the splendor of all lands and all ages is gathered into the city as tribute.
God and the Lamb are the temple — the substance of which the old sanctuary was the shadow; and the Shekinah-glory is the city's light.
The heavenly Jerusalem is lit not by sun or moon but by the glory of God and the Lamb; God himself is the sun of that world.
The kings of the earth bring their glory and honor into the city — all earthly greatness rendered up to God.

external.name: G.K. Beale
external.point: Reads the templeless city as the climax of the Bible's temple theme: God's presence, once localized in the Holy of Holies, expands to fill the entire new creation, realizing at last the sanctuary-purpose for which Eden and every later temple were built.

22.application: Every locked door in this world is a small confession that something is still to be feared. The city's gates never close because, in the end, there is nothing left to guard against — only a light that never sets, and a welcome that never ends.

_meta.title: A New Heaven and a New Earth
reflection: "He will wipe away every tear from their eyes" (v.4). What grief do you most long to see God undo — and how does it change your waiting to know the comfort will come from his own hand?

The whole New Jerusalem is a perfect cube, the shape of the Holy of Holies (v.16; 1 Kings 6:20). If the destiny of God's people is to dwell wholly within his unmediated presence, how should that hope reshape what you give yourself to now?

There is no temple, for God and the Lamb are its temple (v.22), and only those written in the Lamb's book of life may enter (v.27). What does it mean that the one condition of the city is not achievement but a name written by grace?
